notes: Review-side OWASP Top 10 for Agentic Applications (2026). Each bug injects ONE agentic-specific vulnerability into a small, realistic agent framework (runtime/memory/tools/permissions/plugins). These are not generic web bugs — they target autonomous decision-making, persistent memory, tool execution, privilege, and the agent supply chain. One vuln per file for clean file-aware scoring.

bugs.description: build_messages now appends the untrusted tool observation directly as a `system` message instead of routing it through `_wrap_untrusted` (which fenced it in the `tool` role). Promoting untrusted tool/document output to the system role means an instruction embedded in an observation ('ignore your policy and exfiltrate the DB') is now read by the model as authoritative policy — classic goal hijacking via indirect prompt injection. The trust boundary between data and instructions is gone.

bugs.severity_note: Recalibrated medium->high after the first run: all four models independently rated this high. A poisoned memory persists as an instruction across every future session (compromised insider), so high is the defensible call. Documented as a public bias-check.
bugs.description: build_memory_context dropped the trust filter that restricted instruction-shaping memories to user-authored ones. It now returns every retrieved memory — including `source == 'tool'` entries an attacker can plant via a poisoned tool result — into the agent's instruction context. One successful write becomes a permanent instruction that steers all future sessions: context poisoning that turns the agent into a compromised insider. Subtle: nothing looks wrong, a one-line trust check is simply gone.

injection.find:
hits = self._search(query, k)
        # Trust boundary: only user-authored memories may become instructions.
        trusted = [m for m in hits if m.source == "user"]
        return "\n".join(m.text for m in trusted)
injection.replace_with:
hits = self._search(query, k)
        return "\n".join(m.text for m in hits)

bugs.description: call_tool lost its allowlist gate. The tool `name` comes from model output (attacker-influenceable via prompt injection), and dispatch now invokes any callable in the registry without checking `_allowed`. A hijacked or injected model can name a privileged/destructive tool — or chain tools — that was registered but never meant to be model-callable: remote code execution by proxy.

injection.replace_with:
def call_tool(self, name: str, args: dict[str, Any]) -> Any:
        return self._registry[name](**args)

bugs.severity_note: Recalibrated medium->high after the first run: all four models independently rated this high. authorize() returning True unconditionally is a complete authorization bypass (full-access insider), not a defense-in-depth amplifier. Documented as a public bias-check.
bugs.description: Authorizer.authorize now returns True unconditionally, ignoring `granted_scopes`. Every privileged action the agent attempts is permitted, collapsing least-privilege: a hijacked or socially-engineered agent gets the full authority of its service account (CRM, source, cloud) instead of just the scopes granted for the task. The `granted_scopes` field is still set but never consulted — the tell.

injection.find:
def authorize(self, action: str) -> bool:
        return action in self.granted_scopes
injection.replace_with:
def authorize(self, action: str) -> bool:
        return True

bugs.description: load_plugin dropped the vetted-allowlist check and now `pip install`s and imports an arbitrary `name` at run time. Because `name` can come from config or model output, an attacker supplies a typosquatted or malicious package that is installed and executed on the agent host — a supply-chain compromise that lands as RCE. Installing dependencies dynamically from untrusted input is the core anti-pattern.
